label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's take a look at the given text. The first part of the text starts with several questions, like "Do you start your work by making a list of all you have to complete...?" and others. The author is using these questions to engage the reader and introduce the topic of to-do lists. The next paragraphs then outline various benefits of making to-do lists, such as giving memory a kick, helping stay in the moment, relieving stress, helping with planning, prioritizing, and boosting happiness. The question is asking about how the author introduces the topic. The options are: (A) asking a series of questions, (B) presenting some simple facts, (C) stating his own point of view, or (D) listing the benefits of a to-do list. From the structure of the passage, the author starts with a series of questions to the reader. The main content that follows is about the benefits of to-do lists. But the question is about how the topic is introduced, not what the main content is. Therefore, the introduction method is through the use of rhetorical questions to engage the reader. The correct answer would be (A) asking a series of questions. Let's double-check all the options: - (A) asking a series of questions: Yes, the first part of the text is a series of questions directed at the reader. - (B) presenting some simple facts: The text doesn't start with facts but with questions. - (C) stating his own point of view: The author doesn't state their point of view immediately
